metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `auth_required_scopes` exists (or will exist from S2) in `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']`; if it is a S2 deliverable, add it to the S2-prerequisite list; if it must be added in S5, enumerate it explicitly among the config fields this story introduces.
evidence: grep for 'auth_required_scopes' across entire repo: (no matches)
McpConfig confirmed to have only 3 fields: readonly, allow_llm, allow_jira_sync
_SECTIONS['mcp'] confirmed: only readonly, allow_llm, allow_jira_sync coercers
findings.finding: The AC for scope-testing asserts '403 by a non-empty `auth_required_scopes`' as the mechanism that denies a proxy-authenticated principal with empty scopes. However, `auth_required_scopes` returns zero grep hits in the entire codebase — it is not a registered config key, not in `McpConfig`, and not in `_SECTIONS['mcp']`. If this key is a S2 deliverable, its absence makes the AC's observable oracle unverifiable.
findings.location: AC line 5 — 'denied 403 by a non-empty `auth_required_scopes` (observable oracle: 403 with empty proxy scopes vs 200 once the required scope is granted)'
scenarios: Implementor writes the scope-denial AC test but has no `auth_required_scopes` config key to gate on — test cannot be written as specified.
S2 is supposed to introduce `auth_required_scopes` as part of the composite's audience/scope check, but S2 isn't merged — AC oracle is broken.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the 'What / middleware' bullet what happens to the `X-Forwarded-*` family on a **valid**-secret request (e.g. 'they are also stripped and only the verified identity is propagated via request scope' OR 'they are passed through unchanged'), and add a matching AC assertion for that path.
evidence: 'It strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family (and the configured identity/secret headers) from any inbound request that does not present the valid secret' — valid-secret path behaviour is unspecified
The verifier puts identity into 'a request-scoped principal', so the identity header is not re-emitted — but other X-Forwarded-* headers have no stated fate on a valid-secret path
ABSENCE: no sentence of the form 'on a valid-secret request, X-Forwarded-* headers are [stripped / passed through]'
findings.finding: When the shared secret IS valid, the plan only specifies what headers are stripped on an INVALID-secret request ('strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family … from any inbound request that does not present the valid secret'). It leaves implicit what happens to the X-Forwarded-* family (beyond the identity header itself, which goes into request-scope) on a VALID-secret request: does the middleware pass them through to the MCP app, or does it also strip-and-discard them? An executor could implement either behavior; for a spoofing-surface middleware the choice is security-relevant and must be stated.
findings.location: What section — middleware strip behaviour; Security section — 'strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family'
scenarios: Executor passes all X-Forwarded-* headers through to MCP app on valid-secret requests, leaking upstream network topology or enabling header-based routing attacks downstream
Executor strips all X-Forwarded-* on valid-secret requests, discarding information an operator expects (e.g. X-Forwarded-For for audit logging)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'On a valid-secret request the middleware ALSO strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family (and the secret header) before forwarding; the verified identity is propagated only via the request-scoped principal, never re-emitted as a header.' Then add an AC: 'Unit test asserts that on a valid-secret request, no X-Forwarded-* headers (other than those explicitly re-injected) reach the inner app.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the 'What / middleware' bullet explicitly stating what happens to the `X-Forwarded-*` family (beyond the identity and secret headers) on a **valid**-secret request — e.g. 'they are also stripped and discarded' or 'they are passed through unchanged' — and add a corresponding AC observable oracle that verifies the chosen behavior.
evidence: 'It strips the entire X-Forwarded-*  family (and the configured identity/secret headers) from any inbound request that does not present the valid secret' — the valid-secret path is the missing branch
'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal' — the identity header fate is accounted for, but other X-Forwarded-* headers on the valid-secret path are not
AC item for spoofing (negative controls for each variant) tests only the missing/invalid-secret path, not the valid-secret X-Forwarded-* passthrough
ABSENCE: no sentence of the form 'on a valid-secret request, X-Forwarded-* headers beyond the identity header are [stripped / forwarded as-is]'
findings.finding: On a valid-secret request the plan only specifies that the identity header goes into a request-scoped principal and that the entire X-Forwarded-* family is stripped on INVALID-secret requests; it never states what happens to the broader X-Forwarded-* family (headers beyond the identity/secret headers themselves) when the secret IS valid. Two conformant implementations exist: pass them through (leaking network topology to the MCP app and potentially enabling downstream header-based attacks) or strip them (possibly discarding audit-relevant information). The ACs cover only the invalid-secret negative controls, so neither implementation is caught by the test oracle.
findings.location: What section — middleware bullet; Security section — 'strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family'
scenarios: Executor passes all X-Forwarded-* headers through to the MCP app on valid-secret requests (e.g. X-Forwarded-For, X-Forwarded-Host), leaking upstream topology or enabling downstream header injection
Executor strips all X-Forwarded-* on valid-secret requests, which may break audit logging or IP-based rate limiting downstream, and disagrees with an operator's expectation
Two implementers choose opposite behaviors; neither fails the specified ACs
findings.suggested_fix: In the middleware bullet, append: 'On a valid-secret request the middleware also strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family (and the secret header) from the forwarded ASGI scope, leaving only the verified identity in the request-scoped principal; the identity header itself is not re-emitted as a header.' Add an AC: 'Unit test asserts that on a valid-secret request the X-Forwarded-* headers (beyond the identity header) are absent from the ASGI scope seen by the MCP app.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or expand the S7 scope) requiring an ADR or dedicated security/operator doc entry that captures: (a) the trusted-proxy composite pattern, (b) the operator prerequisite to strip forwarded headers upstream, and (c) what rebar strips on its side — so the safety contract is navigable outside the story plan.
evidence: Plan Security section: 'Operator prerequisite (documented, loud): the fronting proxy MUST overwrite/strip all client-supplied identity and forwarded headers before adding its own'
AC: 'Docs regenerated by S7' — but S7 appears scoped to env-var reference docs, not the security contract
No AC line targets an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or operator runbook entry for the trusted-proxy security model
The phrase 'documented, loud' implies operator-facing documentation is intended, but no plan artifact is identified
findings.finding: The operator security prerequisite ('the fronting proxy MUST overwrite/strip all client-supplied identity and forwarded headers before adding its own') is a safety-critical contract that is mentioned only in the plan's Security section. No doc, ADR, or CLAUDE.md update is planned to surface this to operators who will configure the proxy integration — S7 is scoped to env-var/config docs regeneration, not this architectural security contract.
findings.location: Security section; Acceptance Criteria (no AC for an ADR/doc); plan defers to S7 for docs
scenarios: An operator configures the proxy verifier without reading the plan; the proxy does not strip client-supplied X-Forwarded-User; a client spoofs their identity — the missing doc is the only mitigation gap.
A future maintainer adds a verifier that also reads X-Forwarded-* headers without knowing the stripping contract relies on the middleware being present.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'An ADR or docs/security.md entry captures the trusted-proxy architecture, the operator prerequisite (upstream proxy MUST strip client-supplied identity headers), and rebar's own X-Forwarded-* stripping contract.' Ensure S7 is explicitly scoped to include this, not just env-var table regeneration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the interface mechanism by which the middleware passes a verified proxy identity to the verifier (e.g., 'stored in ASGI scope under key `_rebar_proxy_principal`'), acknowledge it in the design, and add a unit test that verifies concurrent requests do not bleed identity across each other.
evidence: Plan: 'the middleware...extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal' — mechanism unspecified

Plan: 'The verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken' — implies the verifier reads something the middleware wrote, but the interface (ASGI scope key? contextvars? synthetic header?) is not defined
No AC line validates the concurrency behavior of this state-passing mechanism under concurrent requests
ABSENCE: no mention of a named ASGI scope key, no contextvars usage, no interface contract between the two components
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the interface by which the ASGI middleware communicates a verified proxy identity to the verifier component. The choice (ASGI scope dict key, Python context variable, internal synthetic header) has material implications for concurrency correctness and testability, and is an unacknowledged coupling point between two new components.
findings.location: What section — 'The verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken'; Approach & rationale
scenarios: If a threading.local or a bare mutable is used instead of an ASGI scope dict entry or contextvars, concurrent requests could bleed identity across connections.
A future developer adding a second middleware wants to pass different per-request state and does not know the convention, creating two incompatible patterns.
findings.suggested_fix: Name the ASGI scope key (e.g., `scope['_rebar_proxy_principal']`) in the What section; add one AC asserting that two concurrent requests with different identities each receive only their own principal (rules out threading.local misuse).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'strip-then-repopulate' in the Security section with language that matches the actual mechanism: e.g. 'strip-then-place-in-request-scope' (the middleware strips all forwarded/identity headers and places the verified identity in the ASGI request scope, which the verifier reads — no header is re-emitted).
evidence: Security: 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates AND the header survived rebar's strip-then-repopulate'
What/middleware: 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal; otherwise it strips it' — no re-injection into headers is mentioned
What/verifier: 'turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken' — consumes the principal, not a header
findings.finding: The Security section describes the trust model as 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates AND the header survived rebar's strip-then-repopulate,' but the mechanism described in 'What' does not repopulate the identity header back into the request headers — it places the identity into a request-scoped principal consumed by the verifier. The 'repopulate' word implies a header re-injection step that does not exist in the spec, which may cause an implementer to add a spurious header re-injection (making the identity header visible again to the MCP app) or to question the stated mechanism.
findings.location: Security section — 'strip-then-repopulate'; What section — 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal'
scenarios: Implementer interprets 'repopulate' as re-injecting the identity value as a header into the ASGI scope, making the identity header visible to the MCP app (unnecessary and potentially confusing)
Implementer is confused by the contradiction and deviates from the request-scoped-principal mechanism
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates AND the header survived rebar's strip-then-repopulate' to 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates; the middleware strips all X-Forwarded-* and identity headers from the ASGI scope and places the verified identity in request state, which the verifier reads — no identity header is re-emitted to the MCP app.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the 'What' middleware bullet what 'strip-then-repopulate' means on the VALID-secret path: does the middleware re-inject a header, or does it only store the validated identity in ASGI scope state (no header injection)? Co-locate this decision rule with the middleware and verifier descriptions.
evidence: Plan Security: 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates AND the header survived rebar's strip-then-repopulate'
Plan What (middleware bullet): describes stripping on invalid secret, but no 'repopulate' step is described for the valid-secret path
Absence: no description of what 'repopulate' means as an implementation step
findings.finding: The 'strip-then-repopulate' phrase in the Security section implies the middleware re-adds something after stripping on a VALID-secret request, but the 'What' section's middleware description never specifies a repopulation step — only the strip-on-invalid path is described. The distinction matters: if repopulation means re-injecting the identity header downstream, there is an implementation step missing from the spec; if it refers only to storing in ASGI state, the 'repopulate' label is misleading and implies a different (more complex) implementation.
findings.location: Security section — 'identity is trusted ONLY when the secret validates AND the header survived rebar's strip-then-repopulate'
scenarios: An implementer reads 'strip-then-repopulate' as requiring the middleware to strip and then re-add the identity header with a synthetic trusted prefix, adding a header injection step the 'What' spec does not account for

The phrase is actually just shorthand for 'only trust what the middleware validated and stored in scope', in which case no re-injection occurs — but neither reading is authoritative from the plan text

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency-safe carrier for the verified proxy identity (e.g., 'stored in the ASGI `scope` dict under a namespaced key' or 'passed via a `contextvars.ContextVar` reset per request') so the implementation cannot accidentally use shared mutable state.
evidence: 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal' — the term 'request-scoped' is used but the storage mechanism is not named.
The plan does not specify ASGI scope dict, contextvars.ContextVar, or a request-local store as the carrier.
ABSENCE: no mention of 'scope dict', 'contextvars', 'request state', or any concurrency-safe carrier mechanism.
findings.finding: The mechanism by which the ASGI middleware passes the verified principal to the downstream verifier component is unspecified; if implemented via any mutable shared/global variable rather than the per-request ASGI scope dict or a `contextvars.ContextVar`, concurrent requests will race and can cross-contaminate identities.
findings.location: What section — 'the middleware … extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal'; Approach section
scenarios: Under async concurrent requests, if the middleware stores the verified identity in a module-level or instance-level attribute, two concurrent requests can see each other's identities — a principal from request A leaks into the authorization check for request B.
A subtle implementation choice (e.g., `self.verified_identity = ...` on the middleware instance) compiles and passes single-threaded tests but fails under load.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Approach section: 'The middleware stores the verified identity in `scope["rebar.proxy_principal"]` (the ASGI scope dict is per-request and not shared across requests); the verifier reads it from the same key. A ContextVar reset at middleware entry is an acceptable alternative.' This pins the safe implementation pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact ASGI state key (e.g. `scope['state']['proxy_verified_identity']`) or mechanism used to pass the validated proxy identity from the middleware to the verifier, co-located in the 'What' section alongside both components' descriptions.
evidence: Plan: 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal'
Plan: 'The verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken'
No ASGI scope key name, context var, or extension field is named anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The inter-component communication protocol between the ASGI middleware and the proxy verifier is referenced but never specified: the middleware 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal' but the plan never names HOW (ASGI scope state key, a request extension dict entry, a per-request context var, or a re-injected synthetic header). The verifier needs to read exactly this slot; without a named contract the two components cannot be implemented independently and no test can reliably assert the handoff.
findings.location: What section — middleware + verifier bullet points
scenarios: Implementer A puts the identity in scope['state']['proxy_principal']; implementer B reads scope['extensions']['proxy_identity'] — silent incompatibility, tests pass with mocked middleware but fail end-to-end
The AC unit test 'exercises the ASGI middleware + verifier directly' cannot specify the test fixture correctly without knowing the handoff mechanism
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The middleware stores the validated identity string in `scope["state"]["proxy_verified_identity"]`; the verifier reads this key and returns None if absent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `build_server()` in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` as an explicit edit target — it must wrap the FastMCP ASGI app with the proxy middleware when `proxy` is in `auth_strategies`.
evidence: The plan says 'The middleware runs before the MCP app' but only names `mcp_server.py` for its `MCP_ENV_VARS` tuple addition.
Confirmed: `build_server()` (src/rebar/mcp_server.py:241) constructs and returns the FastMCP instance directly with no middleware wrapping today.
Confirmed: `main()` calls `build_server().run()` — middleware must be applied at build time.
findings.finding: `build_server()` in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` is an unenumerated edit target: the plan specifies 'the middleware runs before the MCP app,' which requires `build_server()` to wrap the FastMCP ASGI application with the proxy middleware, but the plan only names `mcp_server.py` in the context of `MCP_ENV_VARS`, not as a function-body edit target.
findings.location: What section: 'Adds the `auth_proxy_*` fields … `MCP_ENV_VARS` (`src/rebar/mcp_server.py`) + `server.json`'; Approach: 'A tiny ASGI middleware is the right seam … runs before the MCP app'
scenarios: An implementer reading the plan edits only `MCP_ENV_VARS` in `mcp_server.py` but does not modify `build_server()` to wrap the FastMCP ASGI app with the middleware, leaving the middleware effectively dead code.
Integration test in AC6 ('httpx ASGITransport asserts an authenticated proxy request → 200') would fail because no middleware is active.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'Edits `build_server()` in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` to detect `proxy` in `mcp.auth_strategies` and wrap the FastMCP ASGI app with the `ProxyMiddleware` before returning.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the startup-time behavior of the proxy verifier when `auth_resource_server_url` is absent or empty: either add it to the fail-closed startup check alongside `auth_proxy_secret_env`, or document the expected error path explicitly.
evidence: Plan: 'The minted AccessToken carries resource=auth_resource_server_url, so it is still subject to the composite audience check'
Plan Security section startup check: only 'The env var named by auth_proxy_secret_env MUST be present and non-empty at startup' — no equivalent requirement for auth_resource_server_url
auth_resource_server_url is not listed as an auth_proxy_* field and its presence is not guaranteed by any named startup gate
findings.finding: The verifier mints `AccessToken` with `resource=auth_resource_server_url`, but the plan specifies no fallback or failure mode for the case where `auth_resource_server_url` is absent or unconfigured. The startup check only covers `auth_proxy_secret_env`; a missing resource URL would cause the minted token to fail the composite audience check silently (401 with no diagnostic), or the verifier might mint a token with `resource=None` that the audience check handles inconsistently.
findings.location: What section — verifier bullet: 'resource=auth_resource_server_url'; Security section — fail-closed startup checks
scenarios: Operator configures `proxy` strategy but forgets `auth_resource_server_url`; the proxy verifier starts successfully (only the secret env var is checked at startup), then every authenticated proxy request gets 401 from the audience check with no error message distinguishing 'no identity' from 'misconfigured resource URL'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The proxy verifier MUST also verify at startup that `auth_resource_server_url` is non-empty; if absent, startup fails with a clear message naming the missing key.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific `FastMCP` attribute or method (e.g. `.asgi_app`, `.streamable_http_app()`) used to extract the raw ASGI callable for middleware wrapping and `httpx ASGITransport` testing, and confirm it exists in `mcp>=1.9`.
evidence: 'Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`) asserts an authenticated proxy request (valid secret + identity header) → 200'
'The **middleware** runs before the MCP app'
build_server() in mcp_server.py returns a FastMCP object; main() calls .run() (stdio). No ASGI extraction surface is named anywhere in the plan or codebase.
Evidence class: Partially-verified — httpx.ASGITransport EXISTS, but the FastMCP ASGI surface it would be pointed at is Unverified.
findings.finding: The plan asserts an integration-test using `httpx ASGITransport` against the proxy middleware, but never names the specific FastMCP API surface used to extract the raw ASGI callable for wrapping. `build_server()` currently returns a `FastMCP` object and `main()` calls `.run()` on it (stdio). The FastMCP attribute or method needed to obtain an HTTP-mode ASGI app (e.g. `.asgi_app`, `.streamable_http_app()`, or similar) is unspecified in the plan. If that surface does not exist or requires additional FastMCP configuration, both the middleware-wrapping mechanism and the `httpx ASGITransport` integration test are blocked.
findings.location: What section (middleware + verifier description) and AC integration test items
scenarios: Implementer writes the middleware but cannot find a FastMCP-supported API to extract the raw ASGI callable, blocking both runtime wrapping and the AC integration tests.
FastMCP exposes ASGI only in HTTP transport mode (not the default stdio mode used by build_server().run()), requiring a non-trivial test harness change that is unplanned.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Approach or AC: 'The middleware is attached by wrapping `FastMCP`'s ASGI application surface (e.g. `mcp.asgi_app` or the HTTP-transport callable); confirm the exact attribute name against the installed mcp>=1.9 API before implementation begins.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability AC: specify that secret-validation failures (invalid or missing secret), header-stripping events, and startup-time config errors each emit a structured log line (e.g. `logger.warning`/`logger.error` with a distinguishing `event=` key) so operators can alert on anomalous 401 rates or startup failures.
evidence: The Security section lists mitigation requirements (constant-time compare, header stripping, fail-closed) but names no log/metric/alert for any of these events.
The Acceptance Criteria contain no observable oracle for security-event logging.
ABSENCE: no 'log', 'metric', 'trace', 'alert', or 'structlog' mention anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation for security-relevant failure events: invalid/missing shared-secret attempts, header-stripping events, and startup errors produce no named log lines, metrics, or alerts, so operators cannot detect spoofing attempts or misconfiguration in production.
findings.location: Security section; AC — no observability AC exists
scenarios: A brute-force or header-smuggling attempt produces no log signal; operators have no visibility into attack traffic.
A misconfigured proxy that never sends the secret causes all requests to silently 401 with no diagnostic log, making the failure hard to distinguish from a network issue.
Startup rejection of a missing env var has no specified log format, so operators may not know which env var to set.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one AC: 'Unit test asserts that a request with an invalid/missing secret emits a structured warning log (`event="proxy_auth_secret_invalid"` or similar); and that startup failure due to a missing env var emits a structured error log before raising.' This gives operators a concrete alerting handle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit Security requirement (and a matching AC or operator-doc note) stating that the proxy→rebar channel MUST be TLS-protected whenever the MCP server is bound to a non-loopback address, and that plaintext HTTP on that hop is not an acceptable deployment configuration.
evidence: Plan quote: 'a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack)' — S1's gate governs the edge-to-rebar channel, not necessarily the proxy→rebar internal channel.
The shared secret is transmitted as a plain HTTP header (auth_proxy_secret_header, default 'x-proxy-auth') with no plan-level requirement that this header travels over TLS when non-loopback.
ABSENCE: no plan line states 'the proxy→rebar channel MUST use TLS in non-loopback deployments' or references a TLS requirement for the internal proxy-to-backend path specifically.
The plan's constant-time compare and stripping mitigations are correct but assume the secret cannot be eavesdropped; that assumption only holds on loopback or a TLS-protected channel.
findings.finding: The plan defers the encryption posture of the proxy→rebar channel entirely to S1's non-loopback gate ('a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate'), but S5 never explicitly states that the proxy→rebar HTTP channel itself must carry TLS when operating in non-loopback mode. The shared-secret header — the sole credential gating identity trust — rides on this channel. If that internal leg is plaintext HTTP (proxy on a separate host or container network), a network-adjacent actor can intercept the secret and replay it to impersonate the proxy, bypassing the entire authentication layer.
findings.location: Security section: 'Loopback bind is the intended deployment (S1 default); a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key.'
scenarios: Proxy and rebar MCP server deployed as separate containers on a plain Docker bridge network (no TLS between them) — attacker on the same network captures the x-proxy-auth header from any request and replays it to inject an arbitrary identity.

Operator follows S1's non-loopback allowlist + 'TLS-at-edge ack' but interprets 'TLS at edge' as HTTPS termination at the public load balancer; the proxy→rebar internal hop remains HTTP — the shared secret is plaintext on the internal network.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, add: 'When the MCP server is bound to a non-loopback address, the proxy→rebar channel MUST be TLS-encrypted; an operator deploying on a plain unencrypted internal network is not a supported configuration (the shared-secret header is a bearer credential and must not travel in cleartext).' Add a corresponding AC: 'Operator docs (or the S7 env-var reference) note that `auth_proxy_secret_header` is a bearer credential requiring TLS-protected transport on non-loopback deployments.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit unit-test AC (or expand AC2) asserting that a request with the secret header present but containing an incorrect value is treated identically to the absent-secret case: identity header is stripped, the verifier returns None, and the response is 401.
evidence: AC2 says: 'without the secret, the identity header is stripped and the request is unauthenticated (401)' — 'without the secret' covers the absent case, not the wrong-value case.

The Security section says 'constant-time compare gates it' — a wrong value must return the same 401/strip outcome, and this should be an explicit test oracle to guard against implementation errors (e.g., early-exit comparison that leaks timing, or a code path that falls through on non-None but invalid secret).
ABSENCE: no AC line mentions a request that includes the secret header with an incorrect/garbage value.
findings.finding: The acceptance criteria test 'no secret' (absent header) but do not explicitly test 'wrong secret' (header present with an incorrect value). For a constant-time-compare gate on a shared secret, the incorrect-secret case is a distinct and security-critical failure path that is not covered by AC2 or AC3.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('without the secret, the identity header is stripped and the request is unauthenticated (401)')
scenarios: An attacker sends the correct header name with a random/guessed value; a buggy implementation that only checks header presence (not value) would trust it; a test for incorrect-value is the only oracle that would catch this.
A typo in the operator-configured secret env var causes every proxy request to fail with an obscure error rather than a clear 401 — a wrong-secret test would surface this at CI time.
findings.suggested_fix: Expand AC2 to three sub-cases: (a) valid secret → AccessToken; (b) secret header absent → 401 + header stripped; (c) secret header present with wrong value → 401 + header stripped (identical behavior to absent, using constant-time compare). Add a corresponding unit test asserting `verify_token` returns `None` and the middleware strips the identity header for case (c).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `auth_proxy_secret_env` (the indirection layer where a config key names the secret env-var) is necessary, or whether defining a direct `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET` env-var (matching the established `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD`/`WEBHOOK_TOKEN` pattern in `opcert_service/app.py` and `review_bot/config.py`) achieves the same 'no plaintext in config' goal with less config surface and consistent operator UX.
evidence: Plan quote: '`auth_proxy_secret_env` (NAME of an env var holding the proxy shared secret — no plaintext secret in config)'
Codebase: `src/rebar/opcert_service/app.py` line 136: `guard = cfg.guard` where `guard` is populated from `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD` directly — no indirection through a config key naming the env var
Codebase: `src/rebar/review_bot/config.py` line 104: `webhook_token = os.environ.get('WEBHOOK_TOKEN', '').strip() or bot_token` — again a directly-named env var, no meta-level config key
ABSENCE: zero grep hits for any `*_env` config key (a key whose value is an env-var name) across `src/rebar/`

The 'no plaintext in config' goal is equally achieved by defining `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET` as the env var directly (matching the established pattern), making `auth_proxy_secret_env` a surplus config-surface key
findings.finding: `auth_proxy_secret_env` introduces an indirect env-var reference pattern (a config key whose value is the NAME of an env-var holding the secret) that does not exist anywhere else in the codebase. Every other secret in the codebase — `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD` (`opcert_service/app.py`), `WEBHOOK_TOKEN` (`review_bot/config.py`), `GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN`, etc. — is a directly-named env-var, not an indirection through a config key. This adds a third level (`config key → env-var name → secret value`) instead of the established two-level pattern (`env-var name → secret value`) and inflates the config surface with a key whose only job is to name another key.
findings.location: What section: '`auth_proxy_secret_env` (NAME of an env var holding the proxy shared secret — no plaintext secret in config)'; Acceptance Criteria first bullet; `_config_schema.py` / `MCP_ENV_VARS`
scenarios: An operator sets `auth_proxy_secret_env = REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET` in rebar.toml, then must ALSO set that env var — two steps and two failure points vs one.
A documentation reader must understand the indirection: config key → env-var name → secret value, adding cognitive overhead and a novel pattern not shared with any other secret in the project.

The CI drift guard (`scripts/check_server_manifest.py`) must advertise `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET_ENV` (the config key's derived env var) rather than the actual secret env var, which may confuse users reading `server.json`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `auth_proxy_secret_env` with a fixed, directly-named env var `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET` (read via `os.environ.get('REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET', '')` at startup, matching `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD`). Remove the config key. The operator sets the secret directly in the env — identical security posture, one fewer config key, and consistent with every other secret in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Probe `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` (and any S1 story deliverables) for the non-loopback gate / allowlist / TLS-at-edge ack that S5 defers to; if absent, either add it to S5's scope or document the missing control explicitly rather than citing it as pre-existing.
evidence: grep for 'non-loopback' in mcp_server.py: (no matches)
grep for 'allowlist' in mcp_server.py: (no matches)
grep for 'bind' in mcp_server.py: (no matches)
grep for 'TLS' in mcp_server.py: (no matches)
mcp_server.py is confirmed stdio-transport only (build_server returns a FastMCP object, no HTTP bind point)
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key' as a pre-existing security control in the MCP server. However, `mcp_server.py` contains no bind logic, no non-loopback gate, no allowlist check, and no TLS-at-edge acknowledgement. Searching `mcp_server.py` for 'non-loopback', 'allowlist', 'bind', and 'TLS' returns zero hits. The plan uses this claimed pre-existing gate to exclude S5 from adding any bind-related security control.
findings.location: Security section — 'a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key'
scenarios: An operator deploys rebar MCP over HTTP (non-stdio) without the proxy secret gate, relying on the claimed S1 non-loopback gate — but that gate does not exist, leaving the forwarded-header spoofing surface open.
Implementor excludes bind-hardening from S5 scope, trusting S1's gate; later audit finds no such gate was ever built.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `auth_resource_server_url` is introduced by S2 or S5; if S2, add it to the verified-S2-prerequisite list; if S5, enumerate it explicitly as an `auth_proxy_*` config field the story must add to `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']`/`MCP_ENV_VARS`.
evidence: grep for 'auth_resource_server_url' in src/rebar: (no matches)
No such field in McpConfig dataclass (confirmed: only readonly, allow_llm, allow_jira_sync)
No such key in _SECTIONS['mcp'] (confirmed from _config_schema.py lines 538-542)
findings.finding: The plan references `auth_resource_server_url` as a pre-existing config key used to populate the `resource` field of the minted `AccessToken` ('the minted `AccessToken` carries `resource=auth_resource_server_url`'), implying this key already exists in the config schema. A grep across the entire repo returns zero matches for `auth_resource_server_url`.
findings.location: What section — 'resource=auth_resource_server_url so it passes the composite audience check'; Security section — same claim
scenarios: Implementor assumes `auth_resource_server_url` is introduced by S2's config schema additions; if S2 hasn't landed, this key doesn't exist and the audience check cannot be wired.
If `auth_resource_server_url` is expected to be added in S5 itself, the plan needs to say so explicitly — currently it implies the key pre-exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/env-vars.md` (regenerated via `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py`) as an explicit edit target in AC1 / the What section, so the env-var registry drift gate is not an unpleasant surprise during implementation.
evidence: Confirmed: `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` scans `src/rebar/**` for literal env-var reads and generates `docs/env-vars.md`.
Confirmed: `test.yml` line 260 and `gerrit-verify.yaml` line 219 run `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` as a CI drift gate.
Confirmed: `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` line 30 registers 'env-var registry drift gate' → 'scripts/gen_env_registry.py'.
The plan's AC1 mentions `check_server_manifest.py` passes but omits the `gen_env_registry.py` drift gate entirely.
findings.finding: `docs/env-vars.md` and `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` form a separate CI drift gate (wired in both `test.yml` and `gerrit-verify.yaml`) that the plan does not enumerate as an affected artifact. The scanner auto-detects literal `os.environ`/`os.getenv` reads — the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_*` config-key env reads in `_mcp_auth.py` will appear in the scan and trigger drift in `docs/env-vars.md` unless it is regenerated.
findings.location: AC1: 'their derived `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_*` env-var names go in `MCP_ENV_VARS` (`src/rebar/mcp_server.py`) + `server.json`; and `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes'
scenarios: The implementer adds literal `os.getenv('REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET_ENV')` reads in `_mcp_auth.py`, the `gen_env_registry.py` scanner detects them, `docs/env-vars.md` becomes stale, and the CI drift gate fails — blocking the build even though the plan's own AC1 claims 'passes'.

If reads are done indirectly (dynamic key via `os.environ.get(config.auth_proxy_secret_env)`) the scanner marks them as 'dynamic' (not auto-detected) and the `docs/env-vars.md` WILL be stale in a different way — the proxied-secret env var itself is never auto-documented.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: 'and `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` is rerun so `docs/env-vars.md` reflects the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_*` env reads (the env-var registry drift gate in CI must also pass).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or verify the CVE references in 'Approach & rationale': confirm CVE-2025-64484 is a real published advisory and remove/correct CVE-2026-40575 (future-year identifier cannot be a published CVE); substitute with the actual advisory URL or a generic 'forwarded-header spoofing' threat description if no published CVE exists.
evidence: Plan text: 'This is the documented mitigation for the forwarded-header-spoofing class (oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484).'
CVE-2026-40575 carries a future year (2026); no such CVE can be publicly published at time of authoring.
Neither CVE string appears anywhere in the codebase, confirming these are external references not yet validated.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'CVE-2026-40575' (a year-2026 CVE identifier) as the documented mitigation rationale for the X-Forwarded-* stripping design. A 2026-year CVE cannot be a published advisory at time of writing; this is either a fabricated identifier or a speculative placeholder. If implementors treat it as authoritative, they may omit verification of the actual threat model or misjudge the severity framing.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section — 'oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484'
scenarios: An implementor looks up CVE-2026-40575 for threat-model detail and finds no published advisory, creating uncertainty about whether the strip-all-X-Forwarded-* scope is correctly calibrated.
A security reviewer accepts the CVE framing at face value and skips an independent threat-model analysis of the header-stripping scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the installed mcp SDK's AccessToken type exposes a 'resource' field and that the composite audience check actually inspects it; document the SDK version and field name in the plan (or in a code comment) so implementors can confirm the capability assertion before coding against it.
evidence: resolve_symbol('AccessToken', module='mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED

Plan text: 'The verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken with client_id=the identity, scopes=auth_proxy_scopes, and resource=auth_resource_server_url so it passes the composite audience check exactly like a token verifier.'
The Security section repeats: 'The minted AccessToken carries resource=auth_resource_server_url, so it is still subject to the composite audience check; fail-closed.'
No existing usage of AccessToken found anywhere in src/rebar — this is a net-new dependency on an SDK type whose shape is unverified.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the minted AccessToken carries 'resource=auth_resource_server_url' and that this field 'passes the composite audience check exactly like a token verifier.' This is a load-bearing security capability assertion — if AccessToken in the installed mcp SDK does not have a resource field, or if the composite does not check it, the audience-bypass protection silently fails. AccessToken is UNRESOLVED in mcp.server.auth in the installed environment.
findings.location: What section — 'the minted AccessToken carries resource=auth_resource_server_url, so it is still subject to the composite audience check'
scenarios: The mcp SDK's AccessToken does not expose a resource field; the proxy verifier compiles but the audience check is never applied, silently bypassing a key security control.
A future mcp SDK upgrade renames or removes the resource field; the bypass goes undetected because no AC explicitly validates the field's presence on the minted token.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key' as a pre-existing codebase seam that covers S5's bind-security boundary. No such gate exists anywhere in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py`, `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, or any other file in `src/rebar/`. The S1 ticket also returns 'not found'. This exclusion deletes the work of specifying how non-loopback deployments are actually constrained, resting S5's security boundary on an unverifiable premise.
findings.location: Security section: 'a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by file/symbol probe) that `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` exists, that `mcp.auth_strategies` is a registered config key in `_SECTIONS['mcp']`, and that the `TokenVerifier` protocol / composite verifier are importable before treating S2 as a closed dependency.
evidence: grep for 'auth_strategies' in src/rebar: (no matches)
grep for 'TokenVerifier' in src/rebar: (no matches)
grep for '_mcp_auth' in src/rebar: (no matches)
grep for 'AccessToken' in src/rebar: (no matches)
list_directory src/rebar shows no _mcp_auth.py file
Plan states: 'the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**, this story's dependency'
Plan states verifier is 'a TokenVerifier-shaped component' — no such shape exists in repo
findings.finding: The plan's foundational scope-exclusion asserts 'the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**' (`mcp.auth_strategies`) and the composite seam (`TokenVerifier`-shaped verifier, composite verifier, `_mcp_auth.py`) already exist — but no such file, class, or config key is present anywhere in the codebase. A grep for `auth_strategies`, `TokenVerifier`, `AccessToken` (in mcp context), and `_mcp_auth` returns zero hits; `list_directory` of `src/rebar/` confirms `_mcp_auth.py` does not exist. S5 entirely depends on these S2 seams to route its verifier through the composite — if S2 is not yet merged, S5 has no foundation to build on.
findings.location: What section / Approach & rationale — 'the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**', 'activated by including `proxy` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list'; Scope section — 'Depends on S2'
scenarios: S5 is picked up for implementation before S2 lands — executor finds no composite to plug into, no _mcp_auth.py to extend, and no auth_strategies key to register against.
S2 is partially merged but `auth_strategies`/composite contract differs from what S5 assumes — silent integration mismatch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit pre-condition check to the story: 'S2 must be merged and `_mcp_auth.py` / `auth_strategies` / composite verifier verifiable in the repo before this story begins implementation.' Alternatively, enumerate S2's deliverables as explicit entry-point contract items S5 is coded against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence rationale in 'Approach & rationale' explaining why uvicorn.middleware.proxy_headers.ProxyHeadersMiddleware (or starlette equivalents) cannot be reused here — specifically that those middlewares trust based on IP, not a shared secret — so the custom seam's novelty is explicitly justified.
evidence: uvicorn.middleware.proxy_headers.ProxyHeadersMiddleware EXISTS at /Users/joeoakhart/rebar/.claude/worktrees/mcp-auth-epic/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/uvicorn/middleware/proxy_headers.py
The plan does not mention ProxyHeadersMiddleware or explain why a custom implementation is needed over the existing Uvicorn/Starlette primitives.

ProxyHeadersMiddleware's trusted_hosts/forwarded_allow_ips model handles the 'trust only from known proxy IPs' concern, but lacks the shared-secret validation the plan requires — this difference is not articulated.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a bespoke ASGI middleware to strip the entire X-Forwarded-* header family without considering uvicorn.middleware.proxy_headers.ProxyHeadersMiddleware, which EXISTS in the installed environment and is specifically designed for proxy header trust (via trusted_hosts/forwarded_allow_ips). The plan provides no rationale for why this existing mechanism is insufficient, raising a potential reinvention concern under T1(a)/(b).
findings.location: What section — 'A tiny ASGI middleware is the right seam because forwarded identity lives on headers the token-only verify_token seam does not see.'
scenarios: An implementor or reviewer unfamiliar with the plan's shared-secret requirement wonders why existing middleware was bypassed, leading to unnecessary debate.
A future maintainer replaces the custom middleware with ProxyHeadersMiddleware without realizing it lacks secret-validation, reintroducing the spoofing surface.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Approach & rationale': 'Uvicorn's ProxyHeadersMiddleware trusts forwarded headers by source IP (forwarded_allow_ips), not by shared secret, and provides no mechanism to strip-then-repopulate based on secret validation; hence a small purpose-built ASGI middleware is required.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
